@wjakob Why is this `PyCapsule_GetContext()` and not `PyCapsule_GetDestructor()`? 

I see it's self-consistent within pybind11, `PyCapsule_SetContext()` is used here:

https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/da104a9efd0357b5c144c67eb641ae0b9e23012d/include/pybind11/pytypes.h#L1846

But I'd expect that the rest of the world handles destructors via `PyCapsule_SetDestructor()` & `PyCapsule_GetDestructor()`. Could this lead to leaks, because we're never calling PyCapsule_GetDestructor() anywhere in pybind11?

I think we still need to update code to handle receiving response in chunks.

Suppose the query response from server is 100 bytes. We know server will append length as `uint16_t` at start. So overall, we expect to get 102 bytes over TCP.

`HandleTcpReceiveAvailable` is called as TCP receives chunks. So it can be called when we only get 20 bytes of data.
- In the code now, I think we read the 20 bytes and try to parse it as query response which will not be correct.
- We need to wait till we get all bytes. Here is my suggestion:
   - Read the first two bytes to determine the expected `length`
   - Check if `aBytesAvailable` is larger than what we expect (`length + sizeof(uint16)`).
   - If so read and then call `CommitReceive()` which lets `Tcp` know that we are done with the received content and it can be freed and/or reused).
   - Otherwise, we don't call `CommitReceive()` and wait for next call of `HandleTcpReceiveAvailable` (`Tcp` will keep the received content for us util we call `CommitReceive()`).

Yes, that is one solution. There is already `.locate_file()` which is not far off, except that its implementation in `PathDistribution` precisely _removes_ the metadata directory that we need in this case.

More generally, I wonder if there is just a tad too much coupling between the `Distribution` and `PathDistribution` classes?

There is a mix of abstract and concrete methods in the `Distribution` base class, and several of the concrete methods seem fairly tightly bound to the `PathDistribution` subclass. For example, the `at()` static method creates and returns a `PathDistribution` instance outright, and the `metadata()`, `entry_points()`, `files()`, and `requires()` methods are all implemented with assumptions as to which files should be available and that the organization should resemble a dist-info or egg-info distribution.

Now, there is (AFAICS) only the one (`PathDistribution`) subclass of `Distribution` available in this project, and I don't really have enough experience with this project or its users to really imagine what _other_ `Distribution` subclasses would or could exist in the wild.

Still, I would suspect that a subclass that was sufficiently different from `PathDistribution` to rather prefer subclassing `Distribution` directly, would then find itself not merely implementing the `read_text()` an `locate_file()` abstract methods, but would probably also need to _re_implement several of `metadata()`, `entry_points()`, `files()`, or `requires()` as well.

Hence, I would raise the question whether some of these methods would be better off with their concrete implementations moved into the `PathDistribution` subclass? (Of course leaving abstract methods behind in `Distribution` where that makes sense.) This would grant these concrete methods direct access to the stuff they need inside `PathDistribution`, instead of having to add more interfaces to `Distribution` for stuff that really only makes sense for `PathDistribution`.

Still, this is a much bigger refactoring than I set out to do, and I would not feel comfortable starting this without consulting you.

I'd go with the Zen of Python on this: Explicit is better than implicit. Users shouldn't have to guess what the default is when it can be right there in the definition.

The only reason to use `None` as a default argument and override it with the actual default in the body of the function is if your default is mutable and could lead to unexpected behaviour, such as a `dict` or `list`. That's the only case I know of where it's standard to go `arg=None` and then set the actual default in the body:

if arg is None:

        # this will work for PageSelectFormField

        exclude = ('page', 'position', 'placeholder', 'language', 'plugin_type')

        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.fields['attributes'].widget = AttributesWidget()

My idea is that if the user knows about a World and even has session data about it, then 99% of the time [1] that World belongs to a Session the user also belongs to.
So if you're connected to a Game and you know session details about that Game's World, we should assume "user in session" is valid, and if that's valid we should be able to not need a password. This follows with:
- Call `multiplayer_listen_to_session` to make the server start sending you session details.
   - This request fails if you're not a member already.
- Call `multiplayer_request_session_update` to get a full entry, though looking at `MultiplayerSessionWindow.create_and_update` we could get away with just passing a session id here, especially since it already requests the update.

[1]: I can only think of "logged out and then logged in as another user" and "kicked out of session" as reasons why that wouldn't happen. Both are rare enough that just handling as failure case seems good enough.

> Having a valid UUID but there is no session for it

If WorldDatabase has session details for a world, then a session absolutely exists, and you have it's id!
And if doesn't (session got deleted? then the world itself also got deleted and other mess), you `multiplayer_listen_to_session` will fail because you're not a member :)

> valid UUID + session exists but you are not in the session

I would just reject completely if you're not a member. Put the session name in the error message if needed, but let the user join via the normal flow. This is a rare case, let's not overcomplicate it.

Vendoring things may be undesired from a legal PoV. In that case you'd have to comply with the license requirements which often demand that the original code is distinguishable from that you add. This is why vendored libs usually live in their own directory, in separate files/subfolders, and you'll have to maintain their licenses+legal notices in prominent places. Plus, adding your modifications on top would need to be clean, probably separate, and/or well-documented.
Also, `pip install` already gets all the deps and is a de-facto standard.
Vendoring implies added maintenance burden: you'll have to manually figure out if the upstream project has any security vulnerabilities, apply fixes and re-release your project quickly (because otherwise, you steal the ability to get security fixes from your users). This is non-automatable, especially not with pieces of code that is not distributed in a standard way. You could solve this by integrating the tool that pip itself uses (and only because it technically cannot rely on the deps for self-bootstapping): https://pypi.org/p/vendoring.
This should be meant as the last resort and all the projects using it eventually move away from this technique whenever possible.
At least two projects I'm involved with that used to rely on vendored deps have moved away from them when it became possible (CherryPy and Ansible).
With vendoring, you'll also run into problems in distro packaging ecosystems, in particular, because they patch projects and de-vendor their deps. For example, if Fedora has a dependency that is already packaged separately, they'd mark it as your dependency, remove your vendored code and apply patches so that your code imports that.

> I'm wondering if uproot should use dask-awkward's implementation of `_PackedArgCallable` rather than rewriting it.

I don't know; this is pretty small and straightforward:

```python
class _PackedArgCallable:
    """Wrap a callable such that packed arguments can be unrolled.
    Inspired by dask.dataframe.io.io._PackedArgCallable.
    """

    def __init__(self, func, args=None, kwargs=None, packed=False):
        self.func, self.args, self.kwargs, self.packed = func, args, kwargs, packed

    def __call__(self, packed_arg):
        if not self.packed:
            packed_arg = (packed_arg,)
        return self.func(*packed_arg, *(self.args or []), **(self.kwargs or {}))
```

I wouldn't say it's *misconfigured*; I think it's a design defect in Gitlab; it's a Git project hosting system first and a CI tool second, so it only knows how to do CI on projects that it itself is hosting, and we have to mirror Toil over to it to get things tested.

If we also mirrored over outside contributors' forks, that would work. And I'm sure we could write a bot to scan the Github PRs and automatically get the relevant commits into Gitlab, if we trust the author not to steal our environment variables (like the Jenkins whitelist). But as far as I can tell Gitlab has no notion of the existence of a PR on Github at all; all it knows how to do in its Github integration is to set the build status on a *commit*.

The relevant issue in Gitlab itself is https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-ee/issues/5667

If we figured this out before we set up Gitlab I probably would have recommended we just build another Jenkins instance instead. But now that we already have a vaguely working CI system, I'm not sure it would be worth it to switch back to simplify this workflow.

As far as I can tell here you are passing `**kwargs` from the user. Nothing from the keyword arguments is coming "dynamically" from the data being loaded. So a user could do something like this:

```
In [1]: from functools import partial

In [2]: def my_func(a=1, b=2):
   ...:     print(a, b)
   ...:

In [3]: pfunc = partial(my_func, a=3)

In [4]: pfunc(b=3)
3 3

In [5]: pfunc(a=2)
2 2

In [6]: pfunc(a=1)
1 2
```

The point being, by using `partial` we can **attach** args and keyword arguments to any function and then pass it as a callback to the blend function. So users could do `stack_with_weights = partial(stack, weight=my_weights)` and then `mscn.blend(blend_function=stack_with_weights)`. This is "better" in the sense that we avoid accepting `**kwargs` in the `blend` method. At the very least, if we don't want users to use `partial` we should make this a `blend_kwargs=None` keyword argument that gets passed if provided so we don't have to use the wildcard `**kwargs`.

> Type annotations are great, but they would mean we are not supporting anything before Python 3.5, right?

That's correct. However, I am not sure whether this is an issue since I am not sure in what environments this project is being used. It is not a library, so it can't break someone's project by not supporting an older Python version.

> Also, should we defer type annotations to apply globally at once in a separate PR rather than introducing one function looking different from the rest?

I do not believe so; there is a notion of "gradual typing" in Python world meaning a slow step by step addition of type hints into a Python project.

One of the reasons for this is that a PR to add all types everywhere AND make mypy happy would be too large. I prefer smaller PRs, easier to review and to think about.

The reason I think this is if we build the wheel on Python 3.3+ using an old version of `setuptools`, the wheel we push to PyPI will never specify a dependency on `mock`. If people install this wheel on Python 2.7 and don't already happen to have `mock`, our tests will break.

I would prefer it if this were written in polyglot style, so nothing that makes explicit reference to `six.PY2`.

That said, this is the wrong thing to do. The original code block was this (I've annotated with comments to make it clear what's happening):

```python
if isinstance(d_str, six.text_type) and as_bytes:
    # If d_str is str/unicode, convert it into bytes
    d_str = d_str.encode('ascii')
elif isinstance(d_str, bytes) and not as_bytes:
    # If d_str is bytes, convert it to str/unicode
    d_str = d_str.decode('ascii')
```

The problem is that I'm expecting to run the same code with `as_bytes=True` and `as_bytes=False`, which means that whatever `d_str` is, it should have to be converted to the other thing half the time. In Python 2 I expect it to be `bytes` and in Python 3 I expect it to be `str`, so *both* of these branches should *already* be hit.

`unicode(b'example')` is the wrong way to convert from bytes to string, the correct way is `b'example'.encode()`, which is what I'm already doing.

Right, this is because we're defining a specification – all of the behaviors in it are abstract since they describe the _expectation_ for a particular operation's effects. Backends are free to implement the actual execution however they see fit, so long as the observable effects match the specification.

In other words, backends are allowed to cheat and not do what the "conceptually" in the spec says they need to do, but only if they can't possibly get caught cheating. Many optimizations rely on this "if a tree falls in the forest but nobody is there to see it" kind of reasoning.

- Python 2 strings don't have encoding, they are basically `bytes` from python3. The encoding depends on where the strings come from. (File, keyboard, or other)
 - Python 3 string are UTF-8 encoded data.
 - If you want to display a string in Python2, you have to know what the encoding is. If you don't, you are out of luck, and will end up displaying computer gibbrish if you have non ascii letters. This is a major problem with python2, and the reason why python3 is a breaking update.
 - In Python3, you know the encoding, so no problem

- Now pickle want to serialise a list of strings. In Python2 pickle can not know anything about encoding, and therefore saves the string as is.
 - In Python3, pickle must create strings as unicode strings, but it has no clue what the encoding of the strings are (the encoding of each string could be different.) Therefore there is a few options:
    - Open all the strings as `bytes`. This is the correct way but then the user has to decode every single string that was pickled.
    - Open all the strings as `ascii`. Works for all ascii strings but fails as soon as a non ascii charater is found. This is the default for pickle.
    - Choose a random encoding and accept that the non-ascii letters might be gibbrish. This is what we do here.

I think they essentially do want `set -e` to break `travis_retry`. This was added in https://github.com/travis-ci/travis-build/pull/1850. Before that PR, they implemented the command similar to what you've done here.

My understanding for the reason they made this change is people were running commands like `travis_retry source some_script.sh` and if `some_script.sh` included `set -e` it would keep running even if a command failed because it was sourced into a conditional which effectively disables `set -e`. Isn't bash great? (An extra fun detail is apparently this behavior doesn't apply to `bash` on macOS.)

I don't think we should worry about this extreme edge case, but I also think the comment no longer applies to our code so we should delete it so we don't spend time in the future trying to understand it like I just did.

Otherwise, this PR LGTM!

There are two categories of log messages added in this PR:
1. Things that ophyd is doing
2. Things that ophyd is asking the control layer to do.

The log messages in category (2), such as this one, might better be done upstream in the control layers. (Caproto already goes it; pyepics could be made to do it.) _But_ since the immediate motivation for this additional logging is to debug a tricky pseudopositioners issue that may involve pyepics, including older versions of pyepics, I think we _should_ add these log messages, provisionally. In the longer term, we may submit a PR to pyepics and then strip these out after a couple of pyepics releases.

I propose issuing all these kinds of log messages from a separate logger named something like `'ophyd_control_layer'` not under the `ophyd.` logger namespace. That will serve two purposes: it will keep the noise down for users because the control layer-related messages will have to be separately turned up, and it will make it easier to specifically remove just these log messages in the future.

Agree that return_code is pretty distinct from the other DbtCliEvent stuff, so I'm down to remove it. However, the next PR in the stack adds another callsite for execute_cli_stream, which increases the need to have everything factored together. That is, we need to do a few things:

1. create a subprocess with some specific arguments / env vars
2. iterate through the stdout of that subprocess, yielding parsed events as we go
3. wait for the subprocess to complete, raising an error (in some cases) based off the return code
4. have the calling function aware of that return code value (so that it can construct a DbtCliOutput)

So we're stuck in this situation where we want the calling function to consume all the yielded events, but also know the return code. The only way we can do this without having some function which handles both yielding DbtCliEvents and return codes is if the calling process is given the responsibility for 1, 3, and 4. These could take the form of two functions (_get_dbt_subprocess() and _cleanup_dbt_subprocess()), but I figured that might be more confusing than anything. So I just updated the return type of `execute_cli_stream` to be Union[DbtCliEvent, int].

After some investigations, my guess is that some renderers do not properly handle [complex text layouts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_text_layout). Chrome and Inkscape seem to do this, while Internet Explorer doesn't.

There have been [some discussions on Pillow's repository](https://github.com/python-pillow/Pillow/issues/1089) to support this, which have been solved by using `raqm`. However, this requires many dependencies, and it is not included by default (see [Pillow's installation guide](https://pillow.readthedocs.io/en/stable/installation.html)). You can check if you Pillow installation has it:

```
from PIL import features
print(features.check("raqm"))
```

If it is the case, then the output will be similar to Chrome, which is probably the correct way to render text. If not, then it will use a simpler strategy (i.e. glyphs are rendered independently).

Here is the output of `to_image` when `raqm`is available:

![bar_radm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11242808/71901882-29bc0c80-3161-11ea-8f02-bf5e26f26441.png)

I think we should not assume that `raqm` is available. And since other renderers are not consistent anyway, it does not make sense to force one of those "styles".

Still, to actually answer your question, it might be possible to drop these complex layouts by stripping some information when embedding the font. The `raqm` feature would therefore need to be explicitly disabled.

However, I believe it is a separate issue, as this has a limited impact on the SVG result. The goal of this PR is not to challenge the current design, which has worked well for years. But, we can indeed open an issue to keep track of this.

> we really do want anyone trying to call this a second time to fail as they are not getting the effect they want (in this case, the effect that every existing component is trying to get but is no longer getting I believe).

So.  The thinking that went into this was the following:

1. for *components* there is no real usecase (outside of Stacks) for components to register actual values in `registerOutputs`.  Indeed, a long time ago, we went to all our components and changed them to just call `.registerOutputs()` (i.e. with no args).
2. `registerOutputs` then simply became the way for a componetn to tell the UI `i'm done initializing`.  This was purely for the UI so we could change the status from "running..." to "complete".

Because of this, and with the introduction of `async components` it became unnecessary for a component to call `.registerOutputs`.  We will call it for them at a time (thanks to JS semantics) we know is after the derived constructor finishes.  

Either:

1. the component is synchronous.  In which case, we still call `.registerOutputs` for them after their constructor finished.
2. the component is async.  In which case (if they override `async initialize()`) we will also run `.registerOutputs` after them.  

The only time we run before htem is if they finish constructing *after* their constructor, and don't use `initialize()`.  And even in that case, all that happens is hte UI says they completed earlier than normal.  

This does not seem like a very bad thing, and i think the wins in the programming model here are worth it for the change in that corner case.

So the test where I saw this threading context switching was in (sync) test_queue_by_queue_client_conn_str_receive_handler_with_autolockrenew where we set AutoLockRenewer max_workers to be 8. 

The flow of the autolockrenewing (at least what I have seen so far):

In AutoLockRenewer:   

The Main Thread initializes a dispatch worker in _init_workers(). In dispatch_worker() since we have more than one worker we use the ThreadPoolExecutor to create tasks for _auto_lock_renew_task(). If the message needs to be renewed we call renew_message_lock() with one of the threads created by the ThreadPoolExecutor. In this specific example 2 threads (call them 1 and 2) enter into this function one after another. 

In the Receiver:

Entering into the SB reciever, renew_message_lock() calls self._renew_locks(). These 2 threads, 1 and 2, enter this function one after another as well. They then both enter into self._mgmt_request_response_with_retry() which calls client.py.

In pyamqp:

In client.py mgmt_request, these two threads both enter one after another (print statements back to back so context switching). Since thread 1 and thread 2 enter back to back, Thread 1 hits a KeyError and tries to create a new mgmt_link. Switching to Thread 2, it also hits the KeyError as Thread1 has not had time to open() the link yet. For both of these we wait for the client.ready() on the mgmt_link and sleep for .5 seconds if it isn't ready.


(In print Statments) 

More threads go through this process all now using existing mgmt_link (but there are 2). Then you hit this error:

`Unexpected error occurred (ValueError('Connection not open.')). Handler shutting down.`
`AttributeError: 'ServiceBusReceivedMessage' object has no attribute 'renew_lock'`
`Connection closed with error: [b'amqp:not-found', b"The session channel '1' cannot be found in connection 'Connection'.", None]
Unexpected error occurred (ValueError('Management Operation error occurred.')). Handler shutting down.`

and also in the stacktrace: 

` while not self._handler.client_ready():
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'client_ready'`

So then I went and compared this against uamqp. While pyamqp initially creates 2 mgmt_links because the one thread doesn't recognize the other mgmt_link being made in time, uamqp uses the same mgmt link for all renew lock operations. By using the lock in pyamqp, we ended up with one exisitng mgmt_link.

>>> @dataclass(frozen=True)

>>> d = {"foo": "bar"}
>>> a = A(d)

The reason for

```python
if not type(counts).__module__.startswith("cupy."):
```

is to let only CuPy and NumPy pass through that step. If `counts` begins life as a Python sequence, Awkward Array, or anything with an `__array__` method, it will get through this block as a CuPy, NumPy, or possibly NumPy masked array (second version, which you want).

We don't yet have an idiom for this, the way we have `to_layout` to turn whatever you have into an Awkward layout node; before supporting TensorFlow, PyTorch tensors, and JAX, we'll need something like `to_nplike`, but that can be corrected later. (The fact that this advertises itself as a hardcoded check for `"cupy."` will make it stick out in a sweep—this will be one of the easy ones to find.)

> Trying to do a cancel-shielded wait for a subprocess isn't too ridiculous – `nursery.__aexit__` is one of the standard use cases for cancel-shielding, and people will want to do nursery-like things for managing subprocesses.

Actually, on further thought, this is still overstating the importance of this edge case. If someone does a cancel-shielded wait for a subprocess, that will mostly work fine, even with my "simplified" code above. The only way this task gets cancelled is if we're crashing, or if the main task has already exited. That means that the only ways a cancel-shielded `Popen.wait` can fail to actually wait are:

* We're in the middle of crashing with a `TrioInternalError`: well, sorry, that means internal invariants have been violated and we're blowing up the world, so it's OK if your task manager doesn't wait for child processes correctly. At this point all guarantees are void.

* If we're not crashing with `TrioInternalError`, and the system task is cancelled, and someone is doing a cancel-shielded `Popen.wait`, then the main task has already exited, so they must be doing it from *inside a system task*. But doing *anything* inside a cancel-shield in a system task is highly dubious, because by the time a system task gets cancelled the world is being torn down around you. Don't do that please.

It's important to make a distinction between error handling (via raising exceptions) and assertions. Handling bad user input or a flaky network connection is the domain of error handling. Checking the internal consistency of code or data structures is the domain of assertions. Such assertions will often look fairly trivial and _should_ be invariant to user input. See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assertion_(software_development)#Comparison_with_error_handling.

Also, I'm not suggesting we add such trivial checks all the time. It's just that these particular ones helped during development and -- much like with a simple unit test -- I'm loathe to remove them now. Assertions can trivially be disabled at runtime for performance. (Which is why they shouldn't be used for error handling, incidentally.)

Dictionaries are mutable; I try to make sure that we don't feed any side-effects upstream or cause any side-effects downstream.

The copy on line 122 is taking a copy of the requested encryption context so that we can modify it without changing the value in the request.

```python
encryption_context = request.encryption_context.copy()
```

Then, on line 124, we modify it. Long-term I would like to refactor that method so that it returns the new encryption context rather than mutating the input, but mutation is how it has always been so I'm leaving that as-is for now to avoid feature-creep.

```python
signing_key = self._generate_signing_key_and_update_encryption_context(algorithm, encryption_context)
```

On line 126, we take a copy of the modified encryption context for later comparison.

```python
expected_encryption_context = encryption_context.copy()
```

Finally, on line 139, we check the encryption context in the final encryption materials against the copy we made on line 126, before we passed it to the keyring, to make sure that the keyring did not modify it.

I would like to suggest a different approach on how to specify errors similar to how [`sigma` is specified in `tweakwcs`](https://github.com/spacetelescope/tweakwcs/blob/572159d005afb716b433b702f5692150be704158/tweakwcs/linearfit.py#L113-L124) and possibly use some standard nomenclature, see i.e. [SAS Descriptions of Statistics of Fit](https://documentation.sas.com/?docsetId=fsug&docsetTarget=n1sm8nk3229ttun187529xtkbtpu.htm&docsetVersion=15.1&locale=en).

For example, suggested parameter: `max_fit_error` would replace both `max_error` and `max_rms`:

- `None` (Default)
- `float` (i.e., just a number `2.0`) - 'maxerr' type by default
- `tuple`: `(2.0, 'maxerr')` or `(2.0, 'mae')`, or `(2.0, 'rmse')`
- a `list` of tuples to indicate that _ALL_ conditions must be met: `[(5.0, 'maxerr'), (2.0, 'rmse')]`

I think this is both more clear and more flexible than using the two parameters: `max_error`, `max_rms` because it is more obvious what is going on and also because it allows easy expansions to support other statistics in the future.

Even if you do not accept this suggestion, you should document (maybe in a `Notes` _section_) what happens (how the logic works) when both are `None` and when _both_ are defined. Without looking at the code for a user would be impossible to figure out what all these parameters do and how.

    def test_leftclick_should_update_parameter(self, qtbot, default_value):

        with mock.patch('test_inputs.BooleanParameter.value',
                new_callable=mock.PropertyMock,
                return_value=default_value) as p:
            bool_input = BooleanInput(bool_param)

            # Clear any call to property 'value' during initialization
            p.reset_mock()

            qtbot.addWidget(bool_input)
            bool_input.show()

            # TODO: fix: fails to toggle on Windows

            bool_input.setValue(not default_value)

            assert bool_input.value() == (not default_value)
            bool_input.parameter # lazy update

```suggestion
def get_conda_env_dir(env_name):
    """Given a environment name like `tf1`, find and validate the corresponding conda directory"""
    conda_prefix = os.environ.get("CONDA_PREFIX")
    if conda_prefix is None:
        raise ValueError("Serve cannot find environment variables installed by conda. Are you sure you are in a conda env?")
    
    # There are two case:
    # 1. We are in conda base env: CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV=base and CONDA_PREFIX=$HOME/anaconda3
    # 2. We are in user created conda env: CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV=$env_name and CONDA_PREFIX=$HOME/anaconda3/envs/$env_name
    if os.environ.get("CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV") == "base":
        # Caller is running in base conda env.
        # Not recommended by conda, but we can still try to support it.
        env_dir = os.path.join(conda_prefix, "envs", env_name)
    else:
        # Now `conda_prefix` should be something like $HOME/anaconda3/envs/$env_name
        # We want to strip the $env_name component
        conda_envs_dir = os.path.split(conda_prefix)[0]
        env_dir = os.path.join(conda_envs_dir, env_name)
    if not os.path.isdir(env_dir):
        raise ValueError("conda env " + env_name + " not found in conda envs directory. Run `conda env list` to verify the name is correct.")
    return env_dir
```

If you notice I bypass the checking of the set number for brp in the btr0 property. this is done because now it can be set higher then 64, I only bypass this if the calculations are done using the `calc_bit_timing` method.

I also have coded in the means for a user to call the `calc_bit_timing` method providing a custom TimingConst subclass and they are able to pass the `BitTiming` instance after doing that and the timing that were calculated previously will stay and not get overridden when constructing the interface. The use case would be for advanced users that may have some kind of a need to tweak timing min and maximums, a great example of this would be a Ginkgo interface, which shares the same API as the CANalyst-II but the BRP parameter has a min of 1 and a max of 1024. It would allow the library to still work properly without having to specifically code in support. 

Here is an example of how an interface would get set up internally to use this feature. I am using the canalyst-ii that i have updated in my other PR this is only for example purposes.

<details>

```python
from ctypes import *
import logging
import platform
from can import BusABC, Message
from typing import Optional, Dict, Union
from ..typechecking import CanFilters
from .. import CanError
from.. import bit_timing

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

VCI_USBCAN2 = 4
STATUS_OK = 0x01
STATUS_ERR = 0x00

ERR_CAN_OVERFLOW = 0x0001
ERR_CAN_ERRALARM = 0x0002
ERR_CAN_PASSIVE = 0x0004
ERR_CAN_LOSE = 0x0008
ERR_CAN_BUSERR = 0x0010

ERR_DEVICEOPENED = 0x0100
ERR_DEVICEOPEN = 0x0200
ERR_DEVICENOTOPEN = 0x0400
ERR_BUFFEROVERFLOW = 0x0800
ERR_DEVICENOTEXIST = 0x1000
ERR_LOADKERNELDLL = 0x2000
ERR_CMDFAILED = 0x4000
ERR_BUFFERCREATE = 0x8000

TIMING_DICT = {
    5000: (0xBF, 0xFF),
    10000: (0x31, 0x1C),
    20000: (0x18, 0x1C),
    33330: (0x09, 0x6F),
    40000: (0x87, 0xFF),
    50000: (0x09, 0x1C),
    66660: (0x04, 0x6F),
    80000: (0x83, 0xFF),
    83330: (0x03, 0x6F),
    100000: (0x04, 0x1C),
    125000: (0x03, 0x1C),
    200000: (0x81, 0xFA),
    250000: (0x01, 0x1C),
    400000: (0x80, 0xFA),
    500000: (0x00, 0x1C),
    666000: (0x80, 0xB6),
    800000: (0x00, 0x16),
    1000000: (0x00, 0x14),
}

try:
    if platform.system() == "Windows":
        CANalystII = WinDLL("./ControlCAN.dll")
    else:
        CANalystII = CDLL("./libcontrolcan.so")
    logger.info("Loaded CANalystII library")
except OSError as e:
    CANalystII = None
    logger.info("Cannot load CANalystII library")


class CANalystIIException(CanError):
    errno = None
    msg = ''

    def __init__(self, msg=None, errno=None):
        if msg is not None:
            self.msg = msg
        if errno is not None:
            self.errno = errno

        CanError.__init__(self)

    def __str__(self):
        return self.msg + ' ' + '({0})'.format(self.errno)


class CANalystIIStartupError(CANalystIIException):
    pass


class CanalystIIBitrateDetectFailed(CANalystIIException):
    msg = 'Failed to detect bitrate.'
    errno = 1000


class CanalystIIChannelInUse(CANalystIIException):
    msg = 'Channel {0} already in use for device {1}'
    errno = 1001

    def __init__(self, device, channel):
        self.msg = self.msg.format(device, channel)
        CANalystIIException.__init__(self)


class CanalystIIInvalidTimings(CANalystIIException):
    msg = 'Timing registers are not set.'
    errno = 1002


class CanalystIIBitrateNotSupported(CANalystIIException):
    msg = 'Bitrate is not supported.'


class VCI_INIT_CONFIG(Structure):
    _fields_ = [
        ("AccCode", c_ulong),
        ("AccMask", c_ulong),
        ("Reserved", c_ulong),
        ("Filter", c_ubyte),
        ("Timing0", c_ubyte),
        ("Timing1", c_ubyte),
        ("Mode", c_ubyte),
    ]


class VCI_CAN_OBJ(Structure):
    _fields_ = [
        ("ID", c_uint),
        ("TimeStamp", c_int),
        ("TimeFlag", c_byte),
        ("SendType", c_byte),
        ("RemoteFlag", c_byte),
        ("ExternFlag", c_byte),
        ("DataLen", c_byte),
        ("Data", c_byte * 8),
        ("Reserved", c_byte * 3),
    ]


class VCI_BOARD_INFO(Structure):
    _fields_ = [
        ('hw_Version', c_ushort),
        ('fw_Version', c_ushort),
        ('dr_Version', c_ushort),
        ('in_Version', c_ushort),
        ('irq_Num', c_ushort),
        ('can_Num', c_byte),
        ('str_Serial_Num', c_char * 20),
        ('str_hw_Type', c_char * 40),
        ('Reserved', c_ushort * 4)
    ]


class VCI_CAN_STATUS(Structure):
    _fields_ = [
        ('ErrInterrupt', c_ubyte),
        ('regMode', c_ubyte),
        ('regStatus', c_ubyte),
        ('regALCapture', c_ubyte),
        ('regECCapture', c_ubyte),
        ('regEWLimit', c_ubyte),
        ('regRECounter', c_ubyte),
        ('regTECounter', c_ubyte),
        ('Reserved', c_ulong)
    ]


class VCI_ERR_INFO(Structure):
    _fields_ = [
        ('ErrCode;', c_uint),
        ('Passive_ErrData', c_byte * 3),
        ('ArLost_ErrData', c_byte)
    ]


class sja1000Const(bit_timing.TimingConst):
    tseg1_min = 1
    tseg1_max = 16
    tseg2_min = 1
    tseg2_max = 8
    sjw_max = 4
    brp_min = 1
    brp_max = 64
    brp_inc = 1
    f_clock = 8000000

_in_use = {}


class CANalystIIBus(BusABC):
    CANalystIIException = CANalystIIException
    CANalystIIStartupError = CANalystIIStartupError
    CanalystIIBitrateDetectFailed = CanalystIIBitrateDetectFailed
    CanalystIIChannelInUse = CanalystIIChannelInUse
    CanalystIIInvalidTimings = CanalystIIInvalidTimings
    CanalystIIBitrateNotSupported = CanalystIIBitrateNotSupported

    def __init__(
        self,
        channel: Union[int, str],
        device: int = 0,
        bitrate: Optional[Union[int, bit_timing.BitTiming]] = None,
        Timing0: Optional[int] = None,
        Timing1: Optional[int] = None,
        can_filters: CanFilters = None,
        **kwargs,
    ):
        """
        CANalystII Interface

        :param channel: channel number to use
        :type channel: int, str

        :param device: device index. If there is more then one adapter the index number
            will be incremented by one for each additional adapter.
        :type device: int

        :param bitrate: CAN network bandwidth (bits/s).
        :type bitrate: int, optional

        :param Timing0: customize the timing register if bitrate is not specified.
        :type Timing0: int, optional

        :param Timing1: customize the timing register if bitrate is not specified.
        :type Timing1: int, optional

        :param can_filters: filters for packet
        :type can_filters: CanFilters

        :raises: RuntimeError, ValueError, CANalystIIStartupError, CanalystIIChannelInUse,
            CanalystIIBitrateDetectFailed, CanalystIIBitrateNotSupported
        """

        if CANalystII is None:
            raise RuntimeError('CANalystII library failed to load.')

        if (
            isinstance(channel, (list, tuple)) or
            (isinstance(channel, str) and not channel.isdigit())
        ):
            raise ValueError(
                'This class no longer supports multiple channels, '
                'create an instance for each channel'
            )

        channel = int(channel)

        super().__init__(channel=channel, can_filters=can_filters, **kwargs)

        self.channel = channel
        self.device = device
        self.channel_info = "CANalyst-II: device {}, channel {}".format(
            device,
            channel
        )

        if device not in _in_use:
            # this has exception catching because not all of the variations
            # of the ControlCAN library have this function available.
            # The ones that do have it available seem to have less issues
            # when resetting the device before opening it.
            try:
                CANalystII.VCI_UsbDeviceReset(VCI_USBCAN2, device, 0)
            except:
                pass

            b_info = VCI_BOARD_INFO()
            status = CANalystII.VCI_ReadBoardInfo(
                VCI_USBCAN2,
                device,
                byref(b_info)
            )

            if status != STATUS_OK:
                logger.error("VCI_ReadBoardInfo Error ({0})".format(status))

            self._b_info = dict(
                hardware_version=b_info.hw_Version,
                firmware_version=b_info.fw_Version,
                driver_version=b_info.dr_Version,
                library_version=b_info.in_Version,
                irq=b_info.irq_Num,
                num_can_interfaces=b_info.can_Num,
                serial_number=str(b_info.str_Serial_Num, encoding='ascii'),
                hardware_type=str(b_info.str_hw_Type, encoding='ascii')
            )

            status = CANalystII.VCI_OpenDevice(VCI_USBCAN2, self.device, 0)

            if status != STATUS_OK:
                raise CANalystIIStartupError(
                    'Unable to open interface, the device number '
                    'supplied may be incorrect',
                    status
                )

            _in_use[device] = []
        else:
            for cb in _in_use[device]:
                if cb.channel == channel:
                    raise CanalystIIChannelInUse(channel,  device)

            self._b_info = _in_use[device][0].board_info

        _in_use[device] += [self]

        init_config = VCI_INIT_CONFIG()
        init_config.AccMask = 0xFFFFFFFF
        init_config.Filter = 1

        # autodetect bitrate
        
        if isinstance(bitrate, bit_timing.BitTiming):
            try:
                Timing0 = bitrate.btr0
                Timing1 = bitrate.btr1
            except ValueError:
                if not bitrate.calc_bit_timing(sja1000Const()):
                    self.shutdown()
                    raise CanalystIIBitrateNotSupported(errno=bitrate.bitrate) 
            
                Timing0 = bitrate.btr0
                Timing1 = bitrate.btr1
            
        if bitrate is None and Timing0 is None and Timing1 is None:
            logger.info('autodetecting bitrate')
            for bitrate, (Timing0, Timing1) in TIMING_DICT.items():
                logger.info('bitrate autodetect: {0}'.format(bitrate))
                init_config.Timing0 = Timing0
                init_config.Timing1 = Timing1

                status = CANalystII.VCI_InitCAN(
                    VCI_USBCAN2,
                    device,
                    channel,
                    byref(init_config)
                )

                if status != STATUS_OK:
                    continue

                status = CANalystII.VCI_StartCAN(
                    VCI_USBCAN2,
                    device,
                    channel
                )

                if status != STATUS_OK:
                    continue

                msg = self.recv(timeout=0.5)

                CANalystII.VCI_ResetCAN(
                    VCI_USBCAN2,
                    device,
                    channel
                )

                if msg is not None:
                    logger.info('bitrate detected: {0}'.format(bitrate))
                    break
            else:
                self.shutdown()
                raise CanalystIIBitrateDetectFailed()

        elif bitrate is not None:
            if bitrate in TIMING_DICT:
                Timing0, Timing1 = TIMING_DICT[bitrate]
            else:
                self.shutdown()
                raise CanalystIIBitrateNotSupported(errno=bitrate)

        elif None in (Timing0, Timing1):
            self.shutdown()
            raise CanalystIIInvalidTimings()

        init_config.Timing0 = Timing0
        init_config.Timing1 = Timing1

        self.init_config = init_config

        status = CANalystII.VCI_InitCAN(
            VCI_USBCAN2,
            device,
            channel,
            byref(self.init_config)
        )

        if status != STATUS_OK:
            self.shutdown()
            raise CANalystIIStartupError(
                "VCI_InitCAN Error, incorrect channel number?",
                status
            )

        status = CANalystII.VCI_StartCAN(
            VCI_USBCAN2,
            device,
            channel
        )

        if status != STATUS_OK:
            logger.error(
                "VCI_StartCAN Error ({0}), device:{1} "
                "channel:{2}".format(status, device, channel)
            )
            logger.info('receiving failed to start, only able to transmit.')

    def board_info(self) -> Dict:
        """
        Adapter Information

        :return: dictionary containing the following keys

            * `hardware_version`: `int`
            * `firmware_version`: `int`
            * `driver_version`: `int`
            * `library_version`: `int`
            * `irq`: `int`
            * `num_can_interfaces`: `int`
            * `serial_number`: `str`
            * `hardware_type`: `str`

        :rtype: dict
        """
        return {key: value for key, value in self._b_info.items()}

    def send(self, msg: Message, timeout=None) -> None:
        """
        Send CAN Frame

        :param msg: message to send
        :type msg: :class:`can.Message`

        :param timeout: timeout is not used here
        :type timeout: None

        :rtype: None

        :raises: `CanError` if unable to send the message
        """

        can_objs = (VCI_CAN_OBJ * 1)()

        can_objs[0].ID = msg.arbitration_id
        can_objs[0].ExternFlag = int(msg.is_extended_id)
        can_objs[0].SendType = 1
        can_objs[0].RemoteFlag = int(msg.is_remote_frame)
        can_objs[0].DataLen = msg.dlc

        for j in range(len(msg.data)):
            can_objs[0].Data[j] = msg.data[j]

        frames_sent = CANalystII.VCI_Transmit(
            VCI_USBCAN2,
            self.device,
            self.channel,
            byref(can_objs),
            1
        )

        if not frames_sent:
            raise CanError(
                'Unable to send message '
                '(device: {0}, channel: {1})'.format(
                    self.device,
                    self.channel
                )
            )

    def status(self) -> Dict:
        """
        CAN Status

        returns a dictionary with the following keys

            * `err_interrupt`
            * `mode`
            * `status`
            * `al_capture`
            * `ec_capture`
            * `ew_limit`
            * `rx_count`
            * `tx_count`

        :rtype: dict
        """
        status = VCI_CAN_STATUS()
        # not all variations of the ControlCAN library have the
        # VCI_ReadCANStatus function
        try:
            CANalystII.VCI_ReadCANStatus(
                VCI_USBCAN2,
                self.device,
                self.channel,
                byref(status)
            )
        except:
            pass

        return dict(
            err_interrupt=status.ErrInterrupt,
            mode=status.regMode,
            status=status.regStatus,
            al_capture=status.regALCapture,
            ec_capture=status.regECCapture,
            ew_limit=status.regEWLimit,
            rx_count=status.regRECounter,
            tx_count=status.regTECounter
        )

    def error_info(self) -> Dict:
        """
        CAN Error

        returns a dictionary with the following keys

        `error_code`
        `passive_err_data`
        `ar_lost_err_data`

        :rtype: dict
        """
        error_info = VCI_ERR_INFO()

        # not all variations of the ControlCAN library have the
        # VCI_ReadErrInfo function
        try:
            CANalystII.VCI_ReadErrInfo(
                VCI_USBCAN2,
                self.device,
                self.channel,
                byref(error_info)
            )
        except:
            pass

        return dict(
            error_code=error_info.ErrCode,
            passive_err_data=error_info.Passive_ErrData,
            ar_lost_err_data=error_info.ArLost_ErrData
        )

    @property
    def available(self) -> int:
        """
        Check the number of frames waiting to be received.

        :return: number of available frames
        :rtype: int
        """

        return CANalystII.VCI_GetReceiveNum(VCI_USBCAN2, self.device, self.channel)

    def _recv_internal(self, timeout):
        raw_message = (VCI_CAN_OBJ * 1)()

        timeout = -1 if timeout is None else int(timeout * 1000)

        recv_count = CANalystII.VCI_Receive(
            VCI_USBCAN2, self.device, self.channel, byref(raw_message), 1, timeout
        )

        if not recv_count:
            return None, False

        message = Message(
            timestamp=raw_message[0].TimeStamp if raw_message[0].TimeFlag else 0.0,
            arbitration_id=raw_message[0].ID,
            is_remote_frame=bool(raw_message[0].RemoteFlag),
            is_extended_id=bool(raw_message[0].ExternFlag),
            channel=self.channel,
            dlc=raw_message[0].DataLen,
            data=bytearray([raw_message[0].Data[i] for i in range(raw_message[0].DataLen)]),
        )

        return message, False

    def flush_tx_buffer(self) -> None:
        CANalystII.VCI_ClearBuffer(VCI_USBCAN2, self.device, self.channel)

    def shutdown(self) -> None:
        # stops receiving new frames
        CANalystII.VCI_ResetCAN(VCI_USBCAN2, self.device, self.channel)

        _in_use[self.device].remove(self)

        if len(_in_use[self.device]) == 0:
            CANalystII.VCI_CloseDevice(VCI_USBCAN2, self.device)
            del _in_use[self.device]
```

> - check here whether canonical serialization of data is the same as being_processed. If so, allow the request, even though workspace_db is being_processed.

This is not achieving proper workspace locking when the same request is created with overlapping page ranges for the same workspace.

> we would need to synchronise over the actual set of pages of each workspace.

This is the way to achieve proper page range locking to prevent collisions on the processor server/worker level, however, also complex to implement. The more I think about it the more problematic it seems when considering error handling.

1) Ideal scenario example: 200 pages workspace, the Workflow Server will create 4 processing requests (50 pages per request) when knowing there are 4 workers of the specified processor. Then each request's `page_id` field will be in the form `start_page_id..end_page_id`. The `being_processed` boolean field will be replaced with a str field `locked_pages` in the form `start_page_id1..end_page_id1,...,start_page_id4..end_page_id4`. Then detecting overlapping single pages or page ranges is achievable to raise errors for the next coming requests. If the ranges don't overlap and match for the same processor, then the workspace would not be considered locked. For other processors, the workspace will still be considered locked. The unlocking of the workspace for pages will then happen based on the internal callbacks to the Processing Server from the Processor Server or Processing Worker when the execution finishes or fails. 

Problem: Still, in the case when either of the two fails to use a callback, say due to a crash, the workspace will be indefinitely locked. Considering a proper timeout for a server or worker is hard.

 2) Another point:
 Considering that `page_id` could be: 
- a single page - `PHYS0001`
- multiple pages separated by a comma - `PHYS0003,PHYS0004,PHYS0005`
- a page range... `PHYS0009..PHYS0015`

And now assuming a potential scenario where 3 processing requests with different `page_id` formats above are passed - the value of `locked_pages` for the 3 requests will be `PHYS0001_PHYS0003,PHYS0004,PHYS0005_PHYS0009..PHYS0015` assuming that the separator will be an underscore. Or just a string list with values `[PHYS0001][PHYS0003,PHYS0004,PHYS0005][PHYS0009..PHYS0015]`.

Potential problem: `locked_pages` is filled with a lot of values in case single page requests are passed for the entire workspace.

previously, the fact that we *planned* to materialize the hourly parent asset would cause us to examine the downstream daily partitioned asset. we'd then notice that the daily asset was missing, and see if it'd make sense to materialize it. we would then conclude that the hourly parent was outdated (as we haven't materialized it yet, we're just planning to), and we'd get a ParentOutdatedAutoMaterializeCondition, causing us to not materialize the asset. Then, once the hourly asset actually was materialized, we'd go through this process again, this time realizing that it was ok to materialize the downstream.

This is fairly confusing for the user (in my opinion) as the timing of us discovering that the daily asset is missing is basically random. Now, we'll only discover that the asset is missing once the hourly parent is actually materialized.

We had some major discussions about this before you joined. The problem with saying "one-hot encoded labels" is that after Cutmix or Mixup that is no longer true. One-hot encoding basically means turning `3` into `[0, 0, 0, 1, 0]` given we have 4 categories. However, due to the nature of Mixup and Cutmix, afterwards the label could look like `[0, 0.5, 0, 0.5, 0]` if an image with label `1` was mixed with one of label `3`. And that can no longer be called "one-hot encoding".

To find better suiting terms, I looked at some loss functions, e.g. [`CrossEntropyLoss`](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss.html?highlight=crossentropyloss#torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss):

> Target: If containing **class indices**, shape ()(), (N)(N) or (N,d1,d2,...,dK)(N,d1​,d2​,...,dK​) with K≥1K≥1 in the case of K-dimensional loss where each value should be between [0,C)[0,C). If containing **class probabilities**, same shape as the input and each value should be between [0,1][0,1].

(emphasis mine)

And this is why I'm using the terms "index based" and "category based" here.

One thing I have been seeing is that we get a lot of PRs to add new parameters to existing methods. For a lot of our methods, `mount_point` is the last parameter, and the impulse is to keep it last and add new parameters before it, or to rearrange parameters in alphabetical order when they correspond to the API parameters, etc.

The problem is that since we don't enforce keyword use, changing the order of parameters will break any existing uses that passed parameters positionally.

So as a result, I keep having to suggest to PR authors that they move the new parameters to the end of the method definition, even if that doesn't look right.

What I'd like to start doing in `hvac` is that we think of this intentionally when adding new methods, and going forward, we start a transition toward the (breaking) change of making most parameters of most methods kw-only, so that we can solve that going forward.

---

So that's where I'm coming from with this change. 

For this function, `path` makes sense to be the first parameter in this and many other calls, `data` _might_ make sense as a second positional (though I think that it might be better to force it as keyword), and anything else, I think should definitely be forced as a keyword.

Assuming no one calls `materialize_file` other than as part of `materialize_directory`, this isn't being removed, just moved :) `fsync` is an instruction to the OS to say "whatever writes may have buffered for this file, make sure they're actually flushed to disk" (it doesn't refer to any particular writes, it refers to "all pending writes"). So _where_ we `fsync` isn't super important semantics-wise.

The reason this is a bunch faster is that most filesystems these days operate roughly as an append-able transaction log, which isn't per-file, so `fsync` tends to be implemented on the OS level not as "flush all pending changes for this file" but "flush all pending changes to all files". What this change effectively does is switch from doing 100 `fsync`s, each of which is slow (because each has new data to flush to disk), to doing 1 `fsync` which is slow, and 99 `fsync`s which the OS notices are no-ops (because there are no pending changes) so skips. It's heavily reliant on OS behaviour as an optimisation, but is totally correct in terms of correctness because we're still calling `fsync` for each file :)

Right. I see several ways we could go:
1. Leave it as is. (Seems like it leaves an easy improvement on the table.)
2. Add my `if`, allowing subsequent calls to return ASAP when the flag is "used".
3. We could additionally have the definitely-moonlighting threads not construct theirs. That is, if on the first pass through, don't construct `local_runner` if we immediately see that `expected` is already pointing at something. That's a bit more work, that maybe could worth if there wind up being lots of moonlighers. But probably not since worst case it's only the number of worker threads.
4. Try to avoid unnecessary construction in the race case: That would basically require another bit and have the "winner" set the bit to "I'm busy constructing the `local_runner`" which others would have to spin-wait on. That actually might be simpler code? Not sure if it's worth it, though.

My preference is for 2 unless you guys think 3 or 4 is worth the complexity.

Well, we need the docs no matter what. To use a source plugin with Pylint has some weird quirks that must be explained, particularly needing to set up your `source root`. 

The docs could live in these 3 places:

1) The help message for `--pylint-source-plugins`.
2) The website.
3) A new target type.

Downside of #1 is that we lose all newlines and formatting due to how `help` renders. Because this explanation is quite involved, that matters.

Downside of #2 is that this is a really, really niche feature. I don't think we want this taking up real estate on the website, beyond a tiny tip box saying "Run foo for instructions."

--

Pinning is supposed to guarantee stability, but then (some) people will invariably go and update the dependencies on their own (usually after a pip-review or something), and then come here and open tickets if/when breakage occurs.

The `assert` is just a way to document assumptions being made about the code which should always be true, and provide a sanity check during development.  By the time code gets into production, turning them should be OK.  If you still think it could happen IRL, it shouldn't be an assert.  Deleting the check altogether, while fine (I wouldn't argue with you if that's what you eventually did), leaves the assumption implicit and opaque to future developers, whereas an `assert` tells future developers, explicitly, "I'm making this assumption."

This is cheeky.  The point here is that right now, pip is often carelessly clobbering things.  That makes the newest thing installed work, but breaks older things.  If you use a solver, on the other hand, then you may not even be able to install the new thing because the solver knows that the new and old things are incompatible.  Some people rely on pip's breaking behavior, and there a dependency solver is a "bad thing," where really the correct idea is that solvers are good things, and they make sure we keep our environments all happy and hunky-dory.

This is an important change/refactor. Before that, the bounds for the remote sources were computed here using the old context and making the distinction between local/remote sources using the source type. Now the bounds are computed in the Source (for local and remote data) in [[w,s],[e,n]] format. But the user can set the bounds in the Source, Layer or Map, so the format could be also a dict. The `_format_bounds` function checks, clamps and formats the bounds to a unique format (array). The `_compute_bounds` function computes the merge of all the bounds for each layer. The result is a more efficient & cleaner code to get the final bounds for the map.

Key question here is whether this alternate is worth preserving - you've effectively made the 'or sequence of strings' no longer valid (previously `_load_library` would take None, str, or List[str] - but AFAICT your intent is to have it only take `None` or `str`). The use-case for multiple strings is to allow a user to have multiple options (e.g. to support multiple OS platforms) but still have some restrictions (e.g. only listing possible library names for libftdi 0.x). The use case for a single string is when the user knows exactly the path / library name they are using (maybe they've built it themselves), and the use-case for None is that it 'just works' in many situations.

Previously the values of `self._lib_search` could be either str or List[str] and these were just transparently passed to `_load_library`, but I think for type consistency in the `_load_library` interface it would be good at this point to still accept `Optional[str]|List[str]` and if it's a `str` then wrap it in a singleton list, so `self._lib_search` is `Optional[List[str]]`

This change speeds up the solver considerable but is too aggressive in filter packages whose dependency specification need to be considered.  With this change if a decency package broadens a spec of top level package the dependency package's specs are never considered, even when that package is encountered for a different top level package with a broader spec that the first top level.  Essentially a tight specification can enforce itself on packages with broader specifications.

For example consider the packages `top`, `middle`, and `bottom`.  There are two version of `top`, one version of `middle` and two version of `bottom`.  The packages have the following dependencies:

`top-1.0` : `middle`, `bottom=1.5`
`top-2.0` : `middle`
`middle-3.1` : `bottom`
`bottom-1.5` : --
`bottom-2.5` : --

The preferred solution to `conda install top` would be: `top-2.0`, `middle-3.1` and `bottom-2.5`.  

With this change, if `top-1.0` appears first in the `add_these_precs2` tuple, then `middle-3.1` is added to `reduced_index2` but its dependency on an unrestricted `bottom` package is not added to `dep_specs` because it would broaden the existing specification of `bottom=1.5`. 

In the next iteration of the loop when the dependencies of `top-2.0` are examined, `middle-3.1` is not considered as it already in `reduced_index2`.  Therefore `bottom-2.5` is never added to `reduced_index2` and the solution will include `bottom-1.5` as it is the only version of the `bottom` package in the reduced index.

Looking at the gcc sources, it seems that `__POWERPC__` is defined on at least BeOS as well:

```C
#if (defined (_ARCH_PPC)        /* AIX */                               \
     || defined (__powerpc__)   /* gcc */                               \
     || defined (__POWERPC__)   /* BEOS */                              \
     || defined (__ppc__)       /* Darwin */                            \
     || (defined (PPC) && ! defined (CPU_FAMILY)) /* gcc 2.7.x GNU&SysV */    \
     || (defined (PPC) && defined (CPU_FAMILY)    /* VxWorks */               \
         && CPU_FAMILY == PPC)
```

So, I would definitely check for both `__POWERPC__` and `__APPLE__` to be safe.

Well, a tasklet is an iterator, so you know it's done when it raises StopIteration.

If a generator function returns a value, that's returned with the StopIteration exception. Legacy also defined a ``Return`` exception so people could do ``raise Return(foo)`` to return a value from a tasklet. My guess is earlier versions of Python didn't support returning from a generator. ``Return`` is no longer necessary but likely to pop up in legacy code, so it gets to stay! 

From our point of view, there's no real distinction between a tasklet just returning a value like a function and raising Return, as it all winds up with us digging the return value out of the StopIteration exception.

If I understand correctly, in this test we have the following situation after step3:
- Alpha (Primary) has 3 IC and started VC
- Beta has 3 IC and started VC
- Gamma has 1 IC 
- Delta has 1 IC
Since Primary stopped ordering, freshness checks lead to start of a VC on all nodes.
So, it makes sense to call the test like `test_vc_finished_when_less_than_quorum_started_including_primary`.

But what if a different combination of nodes restarted, so that Primary doesn't have a quorum of IC?
For example, 
- Alpha and Beta send IC
- Beta is restarted (wait until it's restarted before go to the next step)
- Alpha is restarted (wait until it's restarted before go to the next step)
- Gamma is restarted (wait until it's restarted before go to the next step)
- Delta sends IC
= >
Alpha - 1 IC 
Beta - 1 IC 
Gamma - 1 IC
Delta - 3 IC => starts VC.
As a result, Delta will be in infinite VC.

I think the freshness check can not solve the situation above, so persistence of IC will be needed.

here's kind of the scenarios where you would use one vs. the other
1. I want the whole body on the response, and I want to access it all at once: Here you would call .content, similar to `.body()` on the old responses. You may need to call `.read()` first, to read in the response's bytes to content before
2. I want to iterate over the body: here, i call one of the `iter_` functions. This doesn't read the body into the response, but outputs it as an iterator. As more of an implementation detail: if the response's bytes are already read into `content`, we will iterate over the content in all of the `iter_` functions except `iter_raw` (consistent with httpx), and output them in chunks <= the block size we get from the transport

AFAICT the current state is that no Arrow filesystems do URL-encoding, but fsspec filesystems might, and we need to guard against silent double-encoding if Arrow filesystems start doing automatic URL-encoding... So the options are:
1. URL-encode for every filesystem and have comprehensive filesystem test coverage to make sure that we aren't double-encoding.
2. Have a white list of Arrow filesystems that we URL-encode for, and have comprehensive filesystem test coverage to make sure that e.g. URLs with spaces are supported across all filesystems.
3. Put the onus on the user to encode their URLs, and tweak `_unwrap_protocol()` to be URL-encoding-aware (i.e. don't fail on the protocol prefix matching).

(3) seems to be the easiest to implement and maintain, but the worst UX, while both (1) and (2) will be more of a pain to maintain, but will offer better (Pandas-equivalent) UX. I'm leaning towards (2) right now.

I tried the approach with a simple HTTP server. In fact I am not that convinced after doing the actual implementation. It is true that the dockerized PyPI server is overkill in term of functionalities, but it is still a simple call to Docker, which is equivalent in term of complexity to call `tools/simple_http_server.py`.

However it adds a significant complexity to `snap/local/compile_native_wheels` by adding extra directory creation, copy and chmod, because for the plain HTTP server, wheels needs to be in directory named after the package names, while the PyPI server just needs a plain directory with all the wheels in it, and will dynamically expose the correct PyPI API. Of course we could abstract the creation of each package class as you proposed, but at this point it is also overkill for only two packages.

For these reasons, and since Docker is a runtime dependency anyway here, I prefer to stay with the Docker approach to serve the wheels.

> The reason I ask, is because I see both a method:
> https://github.com/Grokzen/pykwalify/blob/master/pykwalify/errors.py#L94
> 
> and a property:
> https://github.com/Grokzen/pykwalify/blob/master/pykwalify/errors.py#L55

The code that starts on line 94 is written in a tricky style that's IMO a bit hard to read, but it's a property, not a method. I'm going to copy/paste the entire thing here:

```
def msg():
    doc = """ """

    def fget(self):
        return self._msg

    def fset(self, value):
        assert isinstance(value, basestring), "argument is not string"
        self._msg = value

    return locals()
msg = property(**msg())
```

Notice the final `msg = property(**msg())`. The first definition of `msg`, using `def`, is overwritten immediately by this statement.

So the final value of `msg` is the return value of `property()`, whose arguments are taken by passing the return value of `msg()` as kwargs.

In turn, the `msg()` call returns the dictionary `locals()`, which looks like this:

```
>>> msg()
{'doc': ' ',
 'fget': <function __main__.msg.<locals>.fget(self)>,
 'fset': <function __main__.msg.<locals>.fset(self, value)>}
```

So it's as if you defined

```
msg = property(doc=' ', fget=<some function>, fset=<another function>)
```

and its final value is a "real" property with a getter and a setter function. That's why errors.py on line 55 can treat it like a property; it is one.

I see what Allan is saying about `step` versus `aspect`. Formerly there were `receiving` and `runtime` that could be interpreted as steps or aspects. They could be viewed as steps if they are intended to be separate stages, but they could also be aspects if we say `runtime` includes `receiving`. I think the latter muddies intuitive interpretation.

Perhaps I would ask what the DE definitions of `aspect` and `step` are so we can consistently use them.

To me the intuitive use of aspect would be to in terms of perception as in the case of art, and user experience. The user-aspect would be metrics that reflect how a user perceives something. Another aspect might be how a speaker with a non-optimal decoder perceives transcription. Another still might be transformations on the data to reflect some system aspect of the system (such as the memory requirements of the chunks of audio transcription)  to help us perceive what the decoder experiences as it has different experiences digesting information.

For steps, the term to me denotes something more discrete with beginning and ends as opposed to sequences. A sequence is definitely composed of steps but steps may be composed of substeps (so we'd need to tighten up the terminology). Like a CPU many things may happen in parallel, but each step is discrete and exclusive to itself. I'd also argue steps are less a matter of perception and more a matter of impersonal measurement.

Returning to `runtime`, `queue` and `receiving`, these feel strongly of `steps` given the discreteness of their nature. Each have well defined beginning and ends.

We actually don't need to subclass the visitor class if we don't rely on any of the parent methods, which is simple to do. I think that way we can change the naming scheme to snake case.

The reason I believe this needs to be fixed is because the methods on the _Feature objects are just thin wrappers and should add no new functionality. There are there to redirect to the `F` kernel, rewrap the result and update the meta-data. Adding new functionality means diverging from the behaviour of `F` equivalent kernels. This is why we agreed that the method should receive maximally all parameters and use those that make sense without throwing errors. You can see this in action on how SegmentationMask handles wrong interpretation values. Here we should do the same here.

I do extensive local development with docker hosting services. It is a very important use case for me. However I've been looking closely at https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-rpinitiated-1_0.html#RPLogout

```
post_logout_redirect_uri - OPTIONAL. URI to which the RP is requesting that the End-User's User Agent be redirected after a logout has been performed. This URI SHOULD use the https scheme and MAY contain port, path, and query parameter components; however, it MAY use the http scheme, provided that the Client Type is confidential, as defined in Section 2.1 of [OAuth 2.0](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-rpinitiated-1_0.html#RFC6749) [RFC6749], and provided the OP allows the use of http RP URIs. The URI MAY use an alternate scheme, such as one that is intended to identify a callback into a native application. The value MUST have been previously registered with the OP, either using the post_logout_redirect_uris Registration parameter or via another mechanism. An id_token_hint is also RECOMMENDED when this parameter is included.
```

technically 'SHOULD' is recommended with caveats... MUST is not used in this situation. So as long as one understands and has carefully weighed the impact it's okay to use HTTP instead of https. 


This is very similar to https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html#AuthRequest

```
REQUIRED. Redirection URI to which the response will be sent. This URI MUST exactly match one of the Redirection URI values for the Client pre-registered at the OpenID Provider, with the matching performed as described in Section 6.2.1 of [[RFC3986]](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html#RFC3986) (Simple String Comparison). When using this flow, the Redirection URI SHOULD use the https scheme; however, it MAY use the http scheme, provided that the Client Type is confidential, as defined in Section 2.1 of OAuth 2.0, and provided the OP allows the use of http Redirection URIs in this case. The Redirection URI MAY use an alternate scheme, such as one that is intended to identify a callback into a native application.
```

The scheme is not validated on the authorization request.  

What is validated on the request is the ALLOWED_REDIRECT_URI_SCHEMES and that the redirect_uri is present in the applications redirect_uris.  I feel like we could remove any explicit check for 'scheme' here and instead validate post_logout_redirect_uri on the application model and show a big warning note near the field saying that http schemes are strongly discouraged by the spec and pointing someone to the examples. 

For now I think we can just remove the https check.

Yeah, so for convenience, I made RowResponse conform to the [Sequence base type](https://docs.python.org/3/library/collections.abc.html#collections-abstract-base-classes), so it can be treated as a standard sequence:
- `len(row_response)`
- `for cell in row_response:`,
- `first = row_response[0]`
- `sample = row_response[0:5]`
- etc

I also wanted to make it partially compatible with the [Mapping base type](https://docs.python.org/3/library/collections.abc.html#collections-abstract-base-classes), to make indexing convenient. In this context, the row_response can be treated as a key/value dictionary, where the keys are families or family/qualifier pairs, and the values are the associated cells:
- `cells = row_response["my_family"]`
- `cells = row_response["my-family, "my-qualifier"]`

I added also implementations for Mapping's  [`keys()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict.keys) [`values()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict.values) and [`items()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict.items) functions to make iteration easier:

```
for family,qualifier in row_response.keys():
   cells = row_response[family,qualifier]

for cells in row_response.values():
   print(cells)

for (family,qualifier), cells in row_response.items():
   print(cells)
```

A solution to avoid the memcopy is to use a [`mmap`]():

```python
import mmap

import torch

data = torch.rand(5)
path = "data.bin"

data.numpy().tofile(path)

with open(path, "r+b") as file:
    buffer = mmap.mmap(file.fileno(), 0)
    restored_data = torch.frombuffer(buffer, dtype=data.dtype)

assert torch.all(restored_data == data)
```

Thanks for confirming my technical understanding of `GenerateSourcesRequest` and `TargetFilesGenerator`.

Per my previous comment here, I think that needing to know what files will be generated ahead of time is of limited utility and creates a maintenance burden.

Currently, an `x_sources` target generator needs to be written once, and will keep up to date as more `x` files are added to a directory. Using a `TargetFilesGenerator` that subsets the outputs from another target would mean the `BUILD` file would need to be updated whenever the contents of the target would change. Admittedly, this would guarantee the best dependency/cache performance, however it would come at the expense of a maintenance burden of the build file, and the build errors that arise due to files not being captured from the outputs would be hard to debug.

On the other hand, the implementation in the PR can glob the outputs of another target, so for cases where _all_ of the outputs are needed, the `BUILD` file only needs to be written once, and remains straightforward. If you would benefit from more fine-grained targets, then you can can add new targets for each subset of files. The user has the option of choosing easy maintenance or fine-grained caching.

At the moment, for most of the use cases I'm imagining

In my view, dependency inference is a nice-to-have for this use case, but not essential: in the `experimental_shell_command`/`experimental_run_in_sandbox` use cases, users have to specify dependencies manually.

As a firmer technical red flag here, for `esc` use cases: Java's dependency inference code in particular would need to run the underlying `esc` process to fulfil [the `SourceFilesRequest`s in its first-party symbol mapping](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/main/src/python/pants/backend/java/dependency_inference/symbol_mapper.py#L45-L47). That would mean _every_ `esc` that fulfils a `_sources` target would need to run before dependency inference can be performed, regardless of whether that dependency is necessary to fulfil the goal.

My preference here is for this idea to be wrapped up in an `experimental` API with minimal surface area, so that we can get a better idea about how this is being used, and then proceed with a deeper change in machinery once we've got a more solid understanding of how it'll be used.

Yes. We will have a single Prefix TLV but it can possibly have two BR sub-TLVs one stable and one not stable. And each sub-TLV can have a bunch of entries (which we want to iterate through and count matching ones).

- If our entry is stable, then we want to find the stable sub-TLV, so the current code `prefixTlv->FindSubTlv<BorderRouterTlv>(mStable);` is behavior we want.

- If our entry itself is not stable, the current code/model counts the matching entries which are also not stable. If we want to change this to count both stable and not stable matches, we need to add another enclosing loop to go through both BR sub-TLVs (stable and not stable sub-TLVs). 
   - This is not terribly complex to add but it is not a single line change. 
   - Note that we cannot just use the `FindSubTlv(void)` flavor instead of `FindSubTlv(mStable)`. This won't really iterate over both sub-TLVs.  `FindSubTlv(void)` will find the "first" matching TLV (ignoring the stable flag).

My goal here has been to avoid adding additional logic and behavior in the code to optimize the case that is not really intended as the main use (i.e., using `Publisher` with prefix entries that are not marked as stable). And if we change it since it would be a new behavior we probably need to also add test-case for it (again for something that is not intended to be used).

Considering more on this, I think  it may be better to change the `Publisher` public methods to only allow stable prefix entries. This help keep the code simple and make the intended use clear. I will change this in the next push.

Fyi,  the sector size is actually 0x200 in the hardware, and the value in the DFP flash algo is incorrect. I verified this with the RM and testing. However, because the flash algo always erases 0x8000 we have to leave the sector size at that value.

And in fact, the flash algo, or at least the LPC55S69 version, has a bug that it will erase 0x8000 bytes started at whatever address you give it, even if that address is not aligned to 0x8000.

(I reported this to the SDK team via Jason Martin a while ago.)
