For sure. What’s awkward is that

```shell
  status="$(python3 -c '
    import sys
    ...
  ')"
```

would fail with an `IndentationError`.\* I personally prefer the current
indentation to the −2-space variant, because the opening and closing
single quote appear at the same level of indentation. For comparison, if
the code were

```shell
  status="$(
    some long command that needs \
        multiple lines
  )"
```

then I think the indentation would be unobjectionable. If you have
strong feelings, though, I’ll happily make this flush-left.

(Another alternative is something like

```shell
  status="$(<<EOF sed 's/^    //' | python3 -
    import sys
    ...
EOF
  )"
```

but (a) that still requires the last line’s `EOF` to be at column 0, and
(b) it’s brittle; moving this into a (sh) function would require
changing the `sed` command. It’s also far less obvious.)

(And, yes, `<<"    EOF" ...` does work, but… ugh.)

\* IMHO, this is one major point against significant whitespace: yes,
code written by and for humans should be properly indented, but
(similarly) it can be easier for machines to [generate poorly formatted
code and then autoformat it][1], and you can’t do that if the poor
formatting is an actual syntax error. (To be fair, Haskell has
significant whitespace and will simply strip any uniform leading
whitespace from all lines, so the problem isn’t fundamental.)

[1]: https://github.com/sourcecred/sourcecred/blob/f934735afc97a6bb5015bec3b3d23f85818c6531/src/graphql/generateFlowTypes.js#L127-L132

There is definitely more overhead with submitting a Ray task (as opposed to a RPC call). That being said I think the extra overhead isn't that big of a deal given that if people are trying to create many, many placement groups, the real bottleneck will all likelihood be in waiting for the cluster to scale to the appropriate size to support all the placement groups. 

> What's the correct use case (if any) for a num_cpus=0 task?

If I look at how protobuf is used in python code for instance, it seems similar to me (but the dependency is inferred rather than explicit).

The weirdest part here is putting the explicit dependency in the source field, but with several advantages. It signals that you may only have dependency to one `dockerfile` target, and it forces you to use either the target, or a file from the workspace, there's no room for both.

Of course, the same can be said about using two different targets for `docker_image`. But the naming of the alternative option here, with an inline or synthetic dockerfile, feels a bit off.
Do we have other cases where there are multiple targets that does semantically the same thing, but with different input sources?

Trying the various options out here for size..

```python
# OPT A
docker_image(
  source="Dockerfile",
  image_tags=["1.0"]
)

generated_docker_image(
  name="name-this",
  repository="my-repo/{name}",
  image_tags=["1.0"],
  instructions=[
    "FROM python:3.9",
    ...
  ]
)

## OR OPT B

docker_image(
  source=":my-dockerfile",
  image_tags=["2.0"]
)

dockerfile(
  name="my-dockerfile",
  instructions=[
    "FROM python:3.9",
    ...
  ]
)

## OR OPT C

docker_image(
  source="Dockerfile",
  image_tags=["1.0"]
)

docker_image(
  name="name-this",
  repository="my-repo/{name}",
  image_tags=["1.0"],
  instructions=[
    "FROM python:3.9",
    ...
  ]
)

docker_image(
  source="Dockerfile",
  image_tags=["1.0"]
  instructions=[
    "FROM python:3.9",
    ...
  ]
)
```

I think I prefer opt `C` if opt `B` is a no-go. That is, that we simply introduce a `instructions` field on the existing `docker_image`, and in case you specify both `source` __and__ `instructions`, you might as well merge them (that is OK, it will become a multistage build then, or if instructions doesn't have a `FROM`, it could be that it simply adds to what's already in the `Dockerfile` from the workspace).
